perturbation.original_text: 6. REMEDIES. RECIPIENT recognizes that the remedy at law for any breach of its obligation under this Agreement will be inadequate, and hence, RECIPIENT agrees that HALO shall be entitled to equitable remedies, including injunction, in the event of breach by RECIPIENT.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. PERIOD OF CONFIDENTIALITY. RECIPIENT agrees that for a period of five (5) years from the effective date of this Agreement, RECIPIENT will not disseminate the confidential information except to it’s employees who will be directly involved in the evaluation of the information, and furthermore RECIPIENT will use the same degree of care (but in no event less than reasonable care) to avoid disclosure or unauthorized use of such information as RECIPIENT employs with respect to confidential information of its own. 6. REMEDIES. RECIPIENT recognizes that the remedy at law for any breach of its obligation under this Agreement will be inadequate, and hence, RECIPIENT agrees that HALO shall be entitled to equitable remedies, including injunction, in the event of breach by RECIPIENT.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the remedies clause into the section describing the confidentiality period obscures the scope and applicability of the remedies available to HALO. By placing it within the clause about confidentiality period, it could be interpreted as only applying to breaches of confidentiality, rather than all breaches of the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 726 concerns the application of remedies for breaches of contract. Moving the Remedies clause into the Period of Confidentiality clause restricts the clause's significance and applicability, suggesting it only applies to breaches of confidentiality, thus misleading the recipient and limiting equitable remedies.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 10. ACTIONS IN PARTICULAR CASES [725a - 871.30] ( Title 10 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 1. Actions for the Foreclosure of Mortgages [725a - 730.5] ( Chapter 1 enacted 1872. ) 726. (a) There can be but one form of action for the recovery of any debt or the enforcement of any right secured by mortgage upon real property or an estate for years therein, which action shall be in accordance with the provisions of this chapter. In the action the court may, by its judgment, direct the sale of the encumbered real property or estate for years therein (or so much of the real property or estate for years as may be necessary), and the application of the proceeds of the sale to the payment of the costs of court, the expenses of levy and sale, and the amount due plaintiff, including, where the mortgage provides for the payment of attorney’s fees, the sum for attorney’s fees as the court shall find reasonable, not exceeding the amount named in the mortgage. (b) The decree for the foreclosure of a mortgage or deed of trust secured by real property or estate for years therein shall declare the amount of the indebtedness or right so secured and, unless judgment for any deficiency there may be between
perturbation.justification: The structural flaw, moving the remedies clause into the confidentiality period clause, creates a legal contradiction. The modified text implies that the remedy described only applies to breaches of the confidentiality clause, not to the entire agreement. The LLM explanation highlights this issue, stating that the change "restricts the clause's significance and applicability, suggesting it only applies to breaches of confidentiality, thus misleading the recipient and limiting equitable remedies." This is a structural flaw (the placement) that affects clarity and enforceability, making it difficult for parties to interpret the full scope of the remedies available under the contract. While the provided law snippet discusses mortgage foreclosures, it isn't relevant to the core issue. However, the LLM explanation and the perturbation explanation clearly indicate a potential misinterpretation and limitation of remedies, fulfilling the conditions for 'Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 5. NO LICENSE OR PATENT RIGHTS. Nothing contained in this Agreement shall be construed as granting any license or right to RECIPIENT by HALO under any patent of the United States or a foreign country or otherwise (except for the evaluation purposes indicated above).
perturbation.changed_text: 4. OWNERSHIP OF INFORMATION. All information furnished to RECIPIENT by HALO shall, unless otherwise specified in writing by HALO, remain the property of HALO; and the written information, and any copies thereof, shall be promptly returned to HALO upon its written or destroyed by RECIPIENT at HALO’s option. 5. NO LICENSE OR PATENT RIGHTS. Nothing contained in this Agreement shall be construed as granting any license or right to RECIPIENT by HALO under any patent of the United States or a foreign country or otherwise (except for the evaluation purposes indicated above).
perturbation.explanation: By placing the clause regarding 'NO LICENSE OR PATENT RIGHTS' within the 'OWNERSHIP OF INFORMATION' section, the agreement could wrongly imply that the clause is an extension of ownership, only relating to HALO's ownership of information. This obscures the broad significance of the patent rights clause, which should function independently.
perturbation.law_explanation: 35 U.S.C. § 261 states that patent rights are distinct and should be readily apparent, while this change obscures and minimizes the importance of these rights. The recipient may misinterpret or disregard the section, believing it is simply an extension of HALO's ownership of information, thus infringing on HALO's patent rights.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 35 U.S. Code § 261 - Ownership; assignment | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 35 U.S. Code § 261 - Ownership; assignment U.S. Code Notes prev | next Subject to the provisions of this title, patents shall have the attributes of personal property. The Patent and Trademark Office shall maintain a register of interests in patents and applications for patents and shall record any document related thereto upon request, and may require a fee therefor. Applications for patent, patents, or any interest therein, shall be assignable in law by an instrument in writing. The applicant, patentee, or his assigns or legal representatives may in like manner grant and convey an exclusive right under his application for patent, or patents, to the whole or any specified part of the United States. A certificate of acknowledgment under the hand and official seal of a person authorized to administer oaths within the United States, or, in a foreign country, of a diplomatic or consular officer of the United States or an officer authorized to administer oaths whose authority is proved by a certificate of a diplomatic or consular officer of the United States, or apostille of an official designated by a foreign country which, by treaty or convention, accords like effect to apostilles of designated officials in the United States, shall be prima facie evidence of the execution of an assignment, grant or conveyance of a patent or application for patent. An interest that constitutes an assignment, grant or conveyance shall be void as against any subsequent purchaser or mortgagee for a valuable consideration, without notice, unless it is recorded in the Patent and Trademark Office within three months from its date or prior to the date of such subsequent purchase or mortgage. (July 19, 1952, ch. 950, 66 Stat. 810 ; Pub. L. 93–596, § 1 , Jan. 2, 1975 , 88 Stat. 1949 ; Pub. L. 97–247, § 14(b) , Aug. 27, 1982

perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a structural flaw by placing the 'NO LICENSE OR PATENT RIGHTS' clause within the 'OWNERSHIP OF INFORMATION' section. This placement weakens the clarity and prominence of the patent rights clause, potentially leading the RECIPIENT to misinterpret its significance as merely an extension of information ownership, as described in the Perturbation Explanation. The Law Explanation states this could infringe on HALO's patent rights, which 35 U.S.C. § 261, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, protects as personal property with assignable rights. By obscuring the independent nature of these rights, the change contradicts the intent of making patent rights clear and enforceable.
